perturbation.original_text: In the event a legal dispute arises concerning this Agreement, the prevailing Party shall recover all court costs, expenses, and reasonable attorney's fees.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event a legal dispute arises concerning this Agreement, neither party shall recover attorney's fees, regardless of the outcome.
perturbation.explanation: This change prevents the prevailing party from recovering attorney's fees, which may contradict statutes or common law principles allowing fee recovery in certain contract disputes.
perturbation.contradicted_law: American Rule regarding attorney's fees and potential state statutes allowing fee recovery in contract disputes.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text prohibits the recovery of attorney's fees, contradicting the 'American Rule' which generally states each party pays its own attorney fees, unless a statute or contract provides otherwise. Numerous state statutes and contractual agreements can allow for fee shifting, making this a contradiction.

Costs and fees (a)(1) Except as otherwise specifically provided by statute, a judgment for costs, as enumerated in section 1920 of this title , but not including the fees and expenses of attorneys, may be awarded to the prevailing party in any civil action brought by or against the United States or any agency or any official of the United States acting in his or her official capacity in an
perturbation.justification: The original text allows the prevailing party to recover attorney's fees, while the changed text explicitly prohibits it. The Law Explanation suggests this contradicts the 'American Rule,' where each party pays its own fees unless a statute or contract provides otherwise. The changed text eliminates the possibility of recovering attorney's fees even if a statute would allow it, thereby creating a contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 28 USC 2412, acknowledges instances where costs and fees can be awarded to the prevailing party, further supporting the contradiction. The changed text directly negates this possibility.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall remain in perpetually and upon request, Recipient will promptly return all data and materials furnished by LSUK , or provide written certification of its destruction and destroy any internal analyses and/or work papers related to these discussions .
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall remain in effect for 30 days. Upon request, Recipient will have 30 days to return all data and materials furnished by LSUK, or provide written certification of its destruction and destroy any internal analyses and/or work papers related to these discussions.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the agreement to 30 days and providing a 30 day window to return confidential information contradicts the reasonable measures required under trade secret law for maintaining secrecy.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. A 30-day lifespan for confidentiality and a 30-day return window may be deemed insufficient to protect trade secrets, especially concerning complex or sensitive information. Courts assess the adequacy of protective measures based on the circumstances.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.— (1) In general.— An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.— (A) In general.— (i) Application.— Based on an affidavit or verified complaint satisfying the requirements of this paragraph, the court may, upon ex parte application but only in extraordinary circumstances, issue an order providing for the seizure of property necessary to prevent the propagation or dissemination of the trade secret that is the subject of the action. (ii) Requirements for issuing order.— The court may not grant an application under clause (i) unless the court finds that it clearly appears from specific facts that— (I) an order issued pursuant to Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or another form of equitable relief would be inadequate to achieve the purpose of this paragraph because the party to which the order would be issued would evade, avoid, or otherwise not comply with such an order; (II) an immediate and irreparable injury will occur if such seizure is not ordered; (III) the harm to the applicant of denying the application outweighs the harm to the legitimate interests of the person against whom seizure would be ordered of granting the application and substantially outweighs the harm to any third parties who may be harmed by such seizure; (IV) the applicant is likely to succeed in showing that— (aa) the information is a trade secret ; and (bb) the person against whom seizure would be ordered— (AA

perturbation.justification: The modified contract creates a legal contradiction because it introduces a limited 30-day timeframe for both the agreement's duration and the return of confidential information. This directly undermines the reasonable measures required to protect trade secrets, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses civil proceedings related to trade secret misappropriation, it emphasizes the importance of preventing the propagation or dissemination of trade secrets, implying the need for robust and ongoing protective measures. The 30-day limit, as introduced, may not allow sufficient time to identify and rectify potential breaches, especially for complex or sensitive information, thus contradicting the reasonable protection requirement implied by the law.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient agrees not to copy, duplicate, disclose or deliver all or any portion of the Confidential Information to a third party or permit any other third party to inspect, copy or duplicate the same except those parties deemed necessary by the undersi gned to evaluate a possible business relationship (including agents, advisors, affiliates, accountants, attorneys, consultants, and lenders).
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient agrees not to copy, duplicate, disclose or deliver all or any portion of the Confidential Information to a third party. This obligation does not apply to disclosures required by law, but Recipient must notify LSUK within 72 hours of such disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: The change limits the allowed third-party disclosures and implements a short 72 hour notification window that may be insufficient for LSUK to take protective action.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Varies by jurisdiction; laws governing mandatory disclosure obligations and reasonable notice periods.
perturbation.law_explanation: While FRCP Rule 5.2 addresses privacy protection for court filings, it exemplifies the broader legal principle that individuals and entities must sometimes disclose information due to legal requirements. The modified NDA language imposes a 72-hour notice period for any legally compelled disclosure. This could be problematic because: (1) 72 hours may be insufficient time for LSUK to seek a protective order or take other legal action to prevent or limit the disclosure, and (2) certain legal mandates (e.g., emergency government requests) may require immediate disclosure without any advance notice. Consequently, the 72-hour notice requirement could impede Recipient's ability to comply with valid legal obligations, potentially exposing them to legal penalties for non-compliance.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 5.2. Privacy Protection For Filings Made with the Court | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 5.2. Privacy Protection For Filings Made with the Court (a) Redacted Filings. Unless the court orders otherwise, in an electronic or paper filing with the court that contains an individual's social-security number, taxpayer-identification number, or birth date, the name of an individual known to be a minor, or a financial-account number, a party or nonparty making the filing may include only: (1) the last four digits of the social-security number and taxpayer-identification number; (2) the year of the individual's birth; (3) the minor's initials; and (4) the last four digits of the financial-account number. (b) Exemptions from the Redaction Requirement. The redaction requirement does not apply to the following: (1) a financial-account number that identifies the property allegedly subject to forfeiture in a forfeiture proceeding; (2) the record of an administrative or agency proceeding; (3) the official record of a state-court proceeding; (4) the record of a court or tribunal, if that record was not subject to the redaction requirement when originally filed; (5) a filing covered by Rule 5.2(c) or (d) ; and (6) a pro se filing in an action brought under 28 U.S.C. §§ 2241 , 2254 , or 2255 . (c) Limitations on Remote Access to Electronic Files; Social-Security Appeals and Immigration Cases. Unless the court orders otherwise, in an action for benefits under the Social Security Act, and in an action or proceeding relating to an order of removal, to relief from removal, or to immigration benefits or detention, access to an electronic file is authorized as follows: (1) the parties and their attorneys may have remote electronic access to any part of the case file, including the administrative record; (2) any other person may have electronic access to the full record at the courthouse, but may

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 72-hour notification window for legally compelled disclosures of confidential information. This creates a potential contradiction because, as the Law Explanation states, certain legal mandates may require immediate disclosure without any advance notice. The Perturbation Explanation indicates that the short notification window may be insufficient for LSUK to take protective action. While the scraped snippets do not directly address specific notification periods, the core issue is that the contractual obligation to provide 72-hour notice could impede the Recipient's ability to comply with valid legal obligations, exposing them to legal penalties for non-compliance, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: That all information, data, and materials furnished , either orally or otherwise by LSUK shall be considered "Confidential Information ," which includes all technical and non -technical info rmation concerning the past, present, and future business p ractices and/ or plans to include , without limitation, LSUK 's highly proprietary automated print on demand book manufacturing process , its processes, features, functions, performance, components, s ubsystems, use, technology (whether owned or licensed), intellectual property, resources, research, innovations, products or service offerings, strategic partners, techniques or processes, software, patent applications, inventions, trade secrets, designs, drawings, engineering, hardware configuration information, marketing, strategies, or studies and all tangible and intangible property of any kind, whether conveyed in writing or orally by LSUK or its representatives to Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: Only information explicitly marked as 'Confidential' in writing at the time of disclosure by LSUK will be considered Confidential Information. Oral disclosures are not covered.
perturbation.explanation: This drastically narrows the scope of confidential information, potentially failing to protect trade secrets or other proprietary data disclosed orally, which is contrary to typical trade secret protection practices.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Trade Secrets Act (applies in most US states)
perturbation.law_explanation: Under the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, a trade secret can be information, regardless of form (including oral), that derives independent economic value from not being generally known and is subject to reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy. By excluding orally disclosed information, the modified text fails to protect potential trade secrets disclosed orally if reasonable efforts are made to maintain secrecy.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (as interpreted by the Law Explanation) by excluding orally disclosed information from being considered confidential, which could include trade secrets. The Law Explanation states that trade secrets can be information regardless of form (including oral). Scraped Law Snippet 1 (California Code, CIV 3426.1) defines 'misappropriation' relating to trade secrets but does not explicitly state that trade secrets can be oral. However, it does specify 'duty to maintain secrecy' which implies that the form of communication is less important than the secrecy itself. By excluding oral disclosures, the modified text creates a loophole that weakens the protection of trade secrets that are disclosed orally but are still subject to reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, as described in the Law Explanation.
